title: Discutindo o papel do Programa Bolsa Família na decisão das eleições presidenciais brasileiras de 2006
authors: Rosa Maria Marques; Marcel Guedes Leite; Áquilas Mendes; Mariana Ribeiro Jansen Ferreira
brief_justification: This paper investigates the causal effect of the Bolsa Família program on Lula's vote share in the 2006 presidential election using municipal-level data from MDS, IBGE, and TSE. The authors run a regression controlling for city characteristics (urban/rural, population size, etc.) and claim the program was responsible for 45% of Lula's total votes, making an explicit causal claim without formal identification strategy. The analysis uses publicly available administrative data aggregated at the municipality level, not survey data, and the research design is a standard linear regression with controls (kitchen sink approach).

title: The Elusive New Middle Class in Brazil
authors: Celia Lessa Kerstenetzky; Christiane Uchôa; Nelson do Valle Silva
brief_justification: This is a descriptive empirical paper that uses survey microdata (POF 2008-2009) to characterize the socio-economic profile of Brazil's so-called 'new middle class' through consumption and living standard indicators. It does not estimate causal effects or seek determinants; rather, it challenges the classification of a particular income stratum as 'middle class' by showing their consumption patterns resemble vulnerable/poor strata more than traditional middle class. No regression models, causal claims, or identification strategies are employed -- the analysis is purely descriptive and classificatory.

title: When is statistical significance not significant?

brief_justification: This is a methodological paper that introduces the p-value statistic and demonstrates, through simulations, replications, and observational data, when statistical significance is not meaningful. The evidence is quantitative (simulations and data demonstrations) with explicit methodology.

title: Benjamin Constant and the Combination of the Freedom of the Ancients with that of the Moderns
brief_justification: This is a political theory article that performs an interpretive reading of Benjamin Constant's writings on ancient and modern liberty. It engages exclusively with textual and philosophical analysis of primary sources and secondary literature in the republican and liberal traditions. There are no data, no quantitative methods, no empirical claims, and no causal inferences. The article's contribution is entirely normative and hermeneutic.

title: The African Agenda of Evangelical Christians in Brazilian Foreign Policy: The Crisis of the Universal Church of the Kingdom of God in Angola
authors: Magno Klein; Henrique Rabello de Carvalho
brief_justification: This is a qualitative case study analyzing how Evangelical Christian groups, particularly the Universal Church of the Kingdom of God (IURD), influenced Brazilian foreign policy toward Africa during the Bolsonaro government. The paper uses the 2019 IURD crisis in Angola as a single case study. It contains no quantitative data, no statistical analysis, no tables or figures, and relies on documentary sources including news reports and secondary literature. The paper implicitly argues that Evangelicals shaped foreign policy but does not employ any formal causal research design or quantitative methods.

title: Entre a Desinstitucionalização e a Resiliência: Participação Institucional no Governo Bolsonaro
authors: Carla de Paiva Bezerra; Debora Rezende de Almeida; Adrian Gurza Lavalle; Monika Dowbor
brief_justification: Mixed-methods study of 103 Brazilian federal participatory councils under Bolsonaro (2019-2022). Uses original legal/administrative dataset to classify councils by normative and activity status across policy areas. Develops a typology of institutional resilience based on design strength and policy community insertion. Primarily descriptive/typological with qualitative comparative logic rather than formal quantitative causal inference. Implicit causal claims about government preferences and institutional design driving differential deinstitutionalization, but no regression models, statistical tests, or formal causal design.

authors: MARCOS TOSTES LAMONICA; SERGIANY DA SILVA LIMA
brief_justification: Empirical macroeconomics paper using time-series data for Brazil with 2SLS and GMM estimators to address endogeneity between productivity and investment/wage variables. Tests a new-developmentalist hypothesis that foreign savings (capital flow) does not promote productive investment. The paper specifies econometric equations, acknowledges simultaneity bias, and uses instrumental variable methods, though the specific instruments and sample size are not detailed in the available metadata. Published in a political economy journal with a Kaldorian/post-Keynesian theoretical framework.

title: Relevant Factors for the Voting Decision in the 2002 Presidential Election: An Analysis of the ESEB (Brazilian Electoral Study) Data
brief_justification: This is a quantitative electoral behavior study of the 2002 Brazilian presidential election using post-electoral survey data (ESEB) and logistic regression models. The paper tests hypotheses about factors driving vote choice (religion, partisanship, ideology, government evaluation, candidate attributes) for four candidates. It uses a kitchen-sink logistic regression approach with multiple blocks of predictors rather than a causal identification strategy. The language uses 'explain' in quotes, suggesting awareness that the associations are not strictly causal, but the framing of 'relevant factors for the voting decision' and 'determinants' implies causal reasoning without formal identification.

title: Perceptions on justice, the judiciary and democracy
brief_justification: This is a descriptive empirical paper that uses original survey data from four Brazilian metropolitan regions to explore citizens' perceptions of justice, trust in the judiciary, and dissatisfaction with democracy. It does not employ a causal research design or make causal claims; rather, it describes and explores attitudinal patterns regarding the judicialization of politics in Brazil. The analysis is based on survey responses but the abstract does not indicate regression modeling, causal inference techniques, or significance testing. The paper's goal is to argue descriptively that discontent with political institutions extends to the judiciary, undermining the normative basis for judicialization.
